props.covers: The display domain at packages/canvas/src/stage/: CanvasStage draws the DOCUMENT — grid surface, world transform, the z-tiered object/connector/section layers — and exposes two overlay slots for everything ephemeral; ObjectShape, grid.ts, and viewport.ts are its core; the read-only viewer face proves the core by construction.

text.insert: The render layer is the presentation side of the canvas engine: given an

and a viewport, it draws objects, connections, sections, and annotations to the screen as a world-space DOM/SVG tree. It owns no editing state of its own — it is the shared renderer both the read-only viewer and the interactive editor mount, driven entirely by props.
is the stage-core renderer for the persisted
. It owns the grid surface and the transformed world layer, applies
, and draws document content rather than assembling editor feedback: section fills, connector paths, non-section objects through
, selected-connection trim, floating section header chips, and the
slot sits after the world layer for caller-owned screen-space feedback.
Structurally the stage keeps document rendering in a small set of core pieces:
dispatches each object through its registered def, and
so sections form the backdrop layer while non-sections preserve schema order above them. The z cake remains the five named tiers described by
: section 0, connector 1, object 2, section header 3, world overlay 4. The selected-connection trim is a separate
, so object bodies can cover connector lines without covering the blue selection affordances.
is inside the transformed world layer at z4;
is untransformed screen space. The stage core imports none of the editor feedback components and does not call
to assemble overlays; the editor face injects the pipeline's
CanvasStage composes the other render-layer modules directly: it calls
once per render to compute the dot-grid CSS background for the stage's root div, and it pulls
from theme/tokens.ts for the grid/guide/highlight CSS variables. What it deliberately no longer does is resolve object colors or carry per-shape CSS: each object's colors resolve inside its def via
(theme/palette.ts role tables), and the stage's embedded style tag is composed as a shared base block plus
The board background stays a fixed light surface (
, D9) regardless of host app theme.
/dot-radius for a zoom-aware dot grid pinned to world coordinates. Starting from
= 8 logical px, it computes the projected step as
. While that projected step is below

by powers of two until the grid is legible again. It intentionally does not halve below the base step at high zoom; zooming in lets dots spread out naturally instead of subdividing into a dense screen-space pattern. Dot radius is handled separately from the density tier: it starts from
The enforced screen-space bound is the minimum:
= 6.5px is what triggers the doubling loop.
= 13px is a reference comfort max; high zoom may exceed it because the implementation deliberately avoids high-zoom halving or subdivision.

survives only as a deprecated re-export. The base step and doubling law still reproduce the FigJam observations in the file comment: zoom 0.305 lands on 32 world px for about 9.8px on screen, and zoom 0.21 stays on the same tier for about 6.7px, matching the measured 6.9px within sampling noise.

props.title: Keep the grid legible via a screen-pixel step band, not a fixed world step

text.insert: A fixed world-space grid step would collapse into sub-pixel noise when zoomed far out. Instead, the grid doubles its world-space step only when the projected pitch would fall below
. It does not halve at high zoom; dots are allowed to spread farther apart rather than forcing a tight subdivision.
remains a comfort reference, not an enforced band.
shrank to four exports in the P1 color cutover:
wins, else the universal stroke), and
. Everything else it used to own is gone:
all died with the legacy tone/paletteToken/tint vocabularies (OBJECT-DEF-OVERHAUL.md D1/D10 — retired; see git history). Color resolution now lives in
over the canonical 10-pick role table in
constants file no longer exists: the theme dispersal co-located every FigJam pixel-parity constant with its consumer. Grid constants live in
; board surface variables and universal shape stroke helpers live in
; object trim metrics live with object defs; connector dash metadata lives in
; connector stroke width, hit width, selected endpoint/bend metrics, label chip metrics, and preview stroke metrics live in
. The rule is locality: constants sit beside the renderer that consumes them unless they are part of shared schema/tooling vocabulary.
The provenance discipline survived the dispersal: the constants remain a hand-written, typed mirror of
and figjam-style-spec.md — pixel-sampled measurements from real FigJam exports — now annotated at each site instead of in one file.

is the strictest example: every one of its 100 hex cells carries an inline comment tagging it as sampled (citing its source table/spec section) or derived (no FigJam sample exists; synthesized in the same visual spirit and flagged for contact-sheet review; the palette-contact-sheet tool is retired — see git history). Values are logical px / plain hexes, already normalized against the source export's 2x scale factor.
Render tests pin the pixel and DOM contracts that editors rely on.
covers the stage/overlay stack, arrow markers, selected endpoint circles, bend pills, and the absence of the retired

covers routed connector paths and labels, selected-only endpoint and bend handles, bend-handle label avoidance/layering, 16/700 label text, palette stroke preservation while selected, and solid/dashed path behavior.

covers select-mode anchor dots, hover-driven Connector Mode dots, 20px offset geometry, below-label visual bounds, hover quick-connect ghost/path, hidden-and-inert select-mode zoom gating, Connector Mode gate bypass, and the cursor token ban. The grid tests stay focused on tier math and background CSS output.
CanvasStage is the stage core. The editor face that wires live interaction state, pointer handling, and trim is covered in
; and the reason for the five-tier z cake is covered in

props.title: Board text is trim: user-select is off at the stage root, re-enabled only inside active text editors

text.insert: Shape labels, section titles, and captions are plain DOM text, so before this guard any drag on the board — drag-select, moving a shape, edge-panning mid-gesture — could sweep a native browser text selection across every label it crossed (nothing called
on pointerdown, and no rule suppressed selection). The stage root now sets

, and every label inherits it: board text behaves like trim, not like document text. The only places selection must work are the active text editors, so each of the four editing surfaces (the slot textarea, section-title input, and connector-label input in
, and the contentEditable sticky editor in
) opts back in with an explicit inline
— required, because contentEditable does NOT exempt itself from an inherited
. Deliberately NOT part of the guard: a
in the stage pointerdown handler. The CSS already covers the bug, and the default mousedown drives focus/blur — clicking the canvas must keep blurring (and thereby committing) an open text editor.
At the stage boundary, connectors are mounted as document content, not as editor feedback:
paths in the connector tier at z1 and
; the dash, label, anchor-dot, preview, and trim component story lives in
is the pure stage-core math shared by the viewer and editor faces.
stores the world point shown at screen origin as
wraps the same helpers for both the editor and the interactive viewer; the editor path re-exports it for compatibility. See
pads the target bounds by 48px by default, computes zoom as
, and centers the bounds in the screen, so fitting never requests zoom past 1.0.
is the D4 view-crop input: it finds one section, gathers that section plus transitive
descendants, and returns their bounds with 32px padding by default. The name predates the W6 container-type removal.
is display-only by construction. Its props are the document,

enables navigation only—wheel zoom, pointer pan, and Fit/+/− controls. There is still no tool prop, mutation callback, selection pipeline, or interaction machine, so read-only behavior remains structural rather than a mode flag on the editor.
The viewer measures its shell with
and computes a fit viewport from the measured size. If
. The static viewer recomputes that fit from its measured size. The interactive viewer seeds the shared navigation hook with the same target and refits when the document or
changes, while ordinary container resizes do not erase a user's chosen pan and zoom. Before measurement, the fallback screen is 960 × 360, or 760 × 320 in compact mode. A
id that resolves to no section renders a warning badge instead of a crop.
, no hover id, no active tool, and default
false, every interaction-gated branch collapses; the only overlay content that can render is
. That import is the recorded layout boundary gap:
exception. The open design question is whether the viewer should show a selection box at all. The latent inconsistency is plain:
to true, so the read-only surface draws pointer-capturing resize handles that are inert because nothing is wired to them.
The default card viewer trim is a
shell with header badges for object count, connector count, annotation count, and document id. It also renders the annotations panel when annotations exist.
removes that document trim for iframe and full-screen hosts;
independently controls the Fit/+/− surface. In either form, the stage shell uses an aspect ratio from

Hosts should disclose interaction progressively: show a static SVG crop in an inline card, mount the interactive read-only viewer only after an explicit expand action, and send editing to the Studio editor as a separate authoring context. The

value is a stable section id shared across all three surfaces, so the preview, expanded viewer, and editor can address the same part of one canonical canvas document. Studio routes and the preview endpoint are documented in
